1.exposition: Paul turns from the church's inner life (ch. 12) to the believer's place in the civil order, and the command is sweeping: let every soul be subject to the governing authorities. Its ground is theological — there is no authority except from God, and those that exist stand appointed by him — so that to resist the ordinance is to resist God and to incur judgment. Yet the argument is no blank cheque for tyranny: the ruler is described by his God-given function, God's servant for your good, a terror to bad conduct and not to good, bearing the sword to execute wrath on the evildoer. Hence the believer submits twice over — not only to avoid wrath but for conscience' sake — and renders what is owed, taxes, revenue, respect, honour, as debts of justice rather than gifts of favour.
1.original_language: The higher powers are exousiai hyperechousai — “authorities that are over one,” the abstract noun standing for the concrete magistrate (grammatical-historical Robertson and Ellicott). That these authorities are tetagmenai (“have been ordained,” v.1) is a military perfect: they stand ranked under God like troops drawn up in order, which is why to resist is antitassō, to set oneself in battle-array against God's own arrangement (v.2). In v.4 the ruler bears the machaira (“sword”) — the short sword of execution, taken by the witnesses as the warrant of the magistrate's coercive power, reaching even to the power over life itself.
1.historical_context: Paul writes to Christians in the empire's own capital, and the counsel is pointed. grammatical-historical Gill and grammatical-historical Clarke recall that the church lay under suspicion as seditious — the more so because many believers were Jews, chafing under the Roman yoke and lately expelled by the edict of Claudius (c. AD 49), with Christians confounded among them. Written under Nero's early and still-moderate reign, before the persecution of AD 64, the passage reflects a more benign moment than Revelation 13's later portrait of Rome as a beast. The command to pay tribute would have fallen amid real tax-unrest in the city, and echoes Jesus' own word, “render to Caesar the things that are Caesar's” (Mark 12:17). Strikingly, the pagan official is three times called God's servant and minister — the very language used of gospel ministers.
1.christ: The submission commanded here is one facet of the living sacrifice of Romans 12:1: the same body offered to God pays its taxes and honours its magistrates. And it has a pattern. Christ himself submitted to a Roman verdict, going to the cross under Pilate's authority — even telling him he would have no power at all “unless it had been given from above” (John 19:11). His was no passive capitulation but the working-out of the Father's purpose within the structures of the age; the believer's civic submission follows the same cruciform shape, bearing the order of this world without being defined by it.

verses.note: Let every soul be subject to the higher powers — the command is universal. eastern Chrysostom insists Paul makes much of this to show that Christ gave his laws “not for the subversion of the commonwealth, but for the better ordering of it,” so the Christian is no rebel; the “every soul” spares no one — “if thou be an Apostle even, or an Evangelist, or a Prophet.” The ground is that all authority is of God. reformed Calvin holds the right of government to be God's ordinance, yet pointedly limits the claim — “power is from God, the abuse of power is from what is evil in men” — Paul treating only of power justly exercised, not of tyranny. grammatical-historical Robertson agrees the apostle argues “not for the divine right of kings” but for government and order against all anarchy; and eastern Theodoret already marks the boundary: obey those in authority “if it be consistent with duty to God,” since opposition to the divine laws cancels the duty to rulers.

Whoever resists the authority resists the ordinance of God, and will incur judgment. grammatical-historical Gill defines the resister as one who “denies that there ought to be such order” and will not be subject in any form; grammatical-historical Barnes narrows it to those who “rise up against government itself, seeking anarchy and confusion.” The distinction is important: what is condemned is the spirit of rebellion against the institution, not the conscientious refusal to obey a command against God — which is itself obedience to the higher authority, “we ought to obey God rather than men” (Acts 5:29).

Rulers are not a terror to good works, but to the evil. grammatical-historical Gill hears Paul meeting the obvious objection — that rulers are often terrible — by describing the office at its proper work: governors “neither do nor ought to be a terror” to those who do good, so the well-behaved subject has nothing to fear and may even “have praise” of the authority. The verse states the magistrate's ideal function, the standard by which rule is to be measured, even where particular rulers fall short of it.

Why the good need not fear and the evil must: he is God's servant for your good... he does not bear the sword in vain. grammatical-historical Gill calls the ruler a divinely commissioned “vicegerent,” appointed for the people's good, and reads the sword as the real power of punishment, which the witnesses take to reach even to capital justice. reformed Calvin notes the office is an “avenger to execute wrath,” God's justice administered publicly — which is precisely why the private believer must never avenge himself (Romans 12:19): the wrath forbidden to the individual is entrusted to the magistrate.

One must be subject, not only to avoid wrath but also for conscience' sake. grammatical-historical Barnes lays out the two motives — that government is appointed by God, and that violation exposes one to punishment — and both bind. But puritan Matthew Henry prizes the second and higher: to obey “not so much formidine poenae, from the fear of punishment, as virtutis amore, from the love of virtue,” so that a common civil duty, done for conscience toward God, becomes acceptable to him. The lower motive restrains; the higher hallows the obedience.

For this reason you also pay taxes, for the authorities are God's ministers, attending continually to this very thing. grammatical-historical Gill notes the payment is itself a proof of subjection, rendered “not for fear of trouble, but for conscience' sake” — a duty, not mere prudence. puritan Matthew Henry adds that by paying tribute the subject both owns the magistrate's authority and “contributes his share to the support of the power” whose benefit he enjoys; to resist while paying would be “to pull down with one hand what you support with the other.”

Render to all their dues: tribute, custom, fear, honour. puritan Matthew Henry reads it as “a lesson of justice and charity” — these are paid “as a debt, not a gift”; grammatical-historical Gill spreads the obligation across every rank, “from the supreme to the lowest,” giving each “whatever belongs to them.” The dues are both material (tribute, custom) and moral (fear, honour), and the same principle that pays the tax owes the reverence — and that closing word, “honour,” becomes the hinge into the one debt that can never be discharged.

witnesses.point: Christ gave his laws not to subvert the commonwealth but to order it better; the Christian is therefore no rebel, and “every soul” — apostle, prophet, or monk — owes this subjection.
The right of government is God's ordinance, but “power is from God, the abuse of power is from what is evil in men”; Paul treats only of power justly exercised, not of tyranny.
What is condemned is rising against government itself; where rulers command idolatry or moral wrong, the higher law holds — “we ought to obey God rather than men.”
We are subject not only “for wrath” but “for conscience' sake” — not so much from fear of punishment as from love of virtue, which makes even a civil duty acceptable to God.

external.name: Douglas J. Moo
external.point: Being subject (hypotassō) to the authorities is not identical with unqualified obedience: because the God who institutes the state stands above it, when government commands what God forbids the believer's submission reaches its limit and must yield to obedience to God.

1.application: Paul neither sacralizes the state nor licenses rebellion; he relativizes it. Government is God's servant, answerable to God and honoured for the good it is meant to do — which frees the Christian at once to be a peaceable citizen and to refuse, at conscience's cost, whatever the God above Caesar forbids.

8.exposition: A single word carries Paul from the tax-office to the heart of the law. Having said “pay everyone what you owe,” he adds one debt that is never marked paid: owe no one anything, except to love one another. Every other obligation can be discharged; love is the perpetual debt, always owing, on which one can only keep the interest current — and in paying it, “the one who loves another has fulfilled the law.” Paul proves this by gathering up the second table of the commandments — no adultery, no murder, no theft, no coveting — and folding them all into one saying, “you shall love your neighbour as yourself.” The logic closes in v.10: love works no ill to a neighbour, and therefore love is the fulfilling of the law — not its abolition, but its very substance.
8.original_language: The hinge is a play on opheilē: the “dues” owed in v.7 and the “owe” of v.8 are the same debt-word (grammatical-historical Ellicott) — pay every debt, but this one stays open. And the climactic term is plērōma: love is the fulfilling of the law. grammatical-historical Ellicott presses the form — it says not merely that love helps a man keep the law, but that where love is present “the law is actually fulfilled”; Robertson glosses it “the filling up, the complement” of the law. Paul's “love your neighbour as yourself” is drawn verbatim from the Greek of Leviticus 19:18.
8.historical_context: Naming love of neighbour as the sum of the law was established Jewish teaching, and Jesus had sealed it as the second great commandment (Matthew 22:39–40; cf. Galatians 5:14). Paul cites the second table — the neighbour-directed commands — and, as grammatical-historical Ellicott and Robertson both note, in an order that places adultery before murder, following the sequence of the Septuagint copies he used (the same order in Luke 18:20 and James 2:11) rather than the Hebrew of Exodus 20:13–17. reformed Calvin observes that the command to honour parents is passed over, Paul reckoning only those duties that concern our conduct toward the neighbour at large.
8.christ: The love that fulfils the law has a face. “God shows his love for us in that while we were still sinners, Christ died for us” (Romans 5:8): the one who obeyed the Father to give himself for the undeserving is both the source and the shape of the neighbour-love Paul commands. What the law could not produce, the Spirit works in those who walk by him, so that “the righteous requirement of the law” is fulfilled in them (Romans 8:4). The debt of love we owe is measured by the love we have already received.

verses.note: Owe no one anything, except to love one another. grammatical-historical Ellicott catches the wordplay — “owe” answers “dues” in v.7: when every tax and reverence is paid, “there will still be one debt unpaid, the universal debt of love,” which “must still remain the root and spring of all your actions.” grammatical-historical Robertson puts it memorably: “this debt can never be paid off, but we should keep the interest paid up.” wesleyan-arminian Wesley calls it “an eternal debt, which can never be sufficiently discharged; but yet if this be rightly performed, it discharges all the rest.” The verse forbids not all lawful borrowing but the leaving of any obligation unmet.

You shall not commit adultery, you shall not murder, you shall not steal, you shall not covet — “summed up in this word, you shall love your neighbour as yourself.” grammatical-historical Gill notes Paul reckons up the second table precisely to show that “so far as a man loves his neighbour, he fulfils the law.” The order — adultery before murder — struck the expositors: grammatical-historical Ellicott and Robertson trace it to the Septuagint sequence Paul knew (as in Luke 18:20 and James 2:11). reformed Calvin marks the omission of the command to honour parents, Paul confining himself to the duties owed the neighbour generally. And the measure of the love is exacting — “as thyself.”

Love works no ill to its neighbour; therefore love is the fulfilling of the law. grammatical-historical Barnes turns it toward life: love “would seek to do him good,” preventing every fraud and crime — “if this law were engraven on every man's heart... what a change would it immediately produce in society.” reformed Calvin argues from the effect — “he who is endued with true love will never entertain the thought of injuring others,” for what does the whole law forbid but harm to the neighbour? wesleyan-arminian Wesley completes the thought: “the same love which restrains from all evil incites us to all good.” Ellicott adds that this is the very love unfolded in 1 Corinthians 13:4–7.

witnesses.point: “Owe” answers the “dues” of v.7 — every debt paid, love alone stays unpaid, the universal debt; and the Greek “fulfilling” means the law is actually accomplished where love is present.
The debt of love “can never be paid off, but we should keep the interest paid up”; love is the “filling up,” the complement, of the law.
Under the word love are contained all the commandments, for one truly loving “will never entertain the thought of injuring others” — the whole law forbids nothing but harm to the neighbour.
Love is an eternal debt never sufficiently discharged; the same love that restrains from all evil incites to all good, and so fulfils the whole law toward the neighbour.

external.name: C.E.B. Cranfield
external.point: Love is not a substitute that dispenses with the commandments but their fulfilment: the concrete precepts tell love what it must and must not do, while love supplies the inward reality the law always aimed at — plērōma, filling the law full rather than emptying it.

8.application: Love is not a mood to be waited for but a debt to be paid, and the commandments spell out its instalments: do not betray, do not harm, do not grasp. The searching question is not “do I feel love?” but “have I today done my neighbour any ill — and all the good I could?”

11.exposition: Paul crowns the ethical section with a reason to act now: knowing the time. The hour has struck to wake from sleep, “for salvation is nearer to us now than when we first believed.” He casts it as the turn of night to dawn — the night is far gone, the day is at hand — so “cast off the works of darkness and put on the armour of light.” The imagery is rising and dressing for the day: walk properly as in the daytime, not in the night's works of revelry and drunkenness, immorality and sensuality, quarrelling and jealousy. And the daylight garment is a person: put on the Lord Jesus Christ, and make no provision for the flesh to gratify its desires. The whole ethic of the letter gathers into one command — be clothed with Christ.
11.original_language: The controlling verb is endyō, “to put on” as a garment — used both of the “armour of light” in v.12 and of Christ himself in v.14. grammatical-historical Barnes explains that “to put on a person” was a known Greek idiom meaning to imbibe his principles, imitate his example, take on his very spirit; Robertson says it plainly — “the Lord Jesus Christ is the garment that we all need” (cf. Galatians 3:27). Against this stands pronoia (“provision, forethought”) for the flesh: the believer is to lay in no calculated supply for its lusts, giving them no fuel to feed upon.
11.historical_context: The urgency is redemptive-historical rather than a datebook: Christ has died and risen, the Spirit is given, and the consummation is the next great event, so the present age is spent in its closing “night.” The night-and-day, sober-and-armed imagery is Paul's own elsewhere (1 Thessalonians 5:5–8; Ephesians 6:11–17). The vices named — revelry, drunkenness, sexual licence, strife — were the marks of pagan night-life, from which the church was to be visibly distinct. And v.14 carries its own history: grammatical-historical Ellicott recalls that this very verse, catching the eye of Augustine in a Milan garden, “had a great effect in leading to his baptism and change of life” (Confessions 8.12).
11.christ: “Put on the Lord Jesus Christ” is the climax of the whole ethical section — not “do these things” but “wear this person.” wesleyan-arminian Wesley marvels that Paul “does not say, Put on purity and sobriety, peacefulness and benevolence; but... all this and a thousand times more at once, in saying, Put on Christ.” It is the language of baptism, in which believers “have put on Christ” (Galatians 3:27); the ethic here is simply the outward wearing of a union already given. The “day” that is at hand is finally the appearing of the Lord Jesus himself — the one who has come, coming again.

verses.note: Knowing the time... it is the hour to wake from sleep, for salvation is nearer now than when we believed. eastern Chrysostom hears Paul rousing sleepers, not by terror but by hope: “the time of judgment is at the doors” — as he told the Corinthians, “the remaining time is short” (1 Corinthians 7:29; cf. Hebrews 10:37). puritan Matthew Henry lists the sleep to be shaken off — the sleep of sin, of carnal security, of spiritual deadness — recalling that “both the wise and foolish virgins slumbered and slept” (Matthew 25:5): “the nearer we are to our centre, the quicker should our motion be.”

The night is far gone, the day is at hand; let us cast off the works of darkness and put on the armour of light. grammatical-historical Robertson pictures it vividly — day-break has “drawn nigh,” and we strip off “the works of darkness as we do our night-clothes.” grammatical-historical Ellicott notes how naturally the figure of night and day slides into that of armour: the Christian's warfare is between light and darkness (cf. Ephesians 6:12), and to “put off” the night-clothes is to “put on” the day's arms. reformed Calvin reads the coming “day” as the glorious brightness of the life to come, already beginning to dawn through the gospel.

Let us walk properly as in the daytime — not in revelling and drunkenness, immorality and sensuality, strife and jealousy. grammatical-historical Barnes catches the force of “as in the day”: live “as if all our conduct were seen,” with nothing to conceal, for the shameful deeds are the deeds of night. reformed Calvin arranges the six sins into three pairs — intemperance, uncleanness, and contention — so foul that “even carnal men are ashamed to commit them before the eyes of men,” and therefore doubly unfit for those who live in God's light. grammatical-historical Robertson notes Paul's favourite metaphor “walk,” and the plurals — “revellings, drunkennesses” — the recurring habits of the old life.

But put on the Lord Jesus Christ, and make no provision for the flesh, to gratify its desires. grammatical-historical Barnes explains “put on a person” as to imitate and be clothed with his spirit (cf. Galatians 3:27); reformed Calvin reads it as being “on every side fortified by the power of his Spirit,” the image of God renewed in us. Then the negative: grammatical-historical Clarke and Ellicott take “make no provision” as giving the flesh no thought or supply that would “excite those fleshly impulses which the Christian should try rather to mortify.” wesleyan-arminian Wesley sums the whole of salvation in the positive command — to put on Christ is to be clothed with “all the graces which were in him.”

witnesses.point: Paul rouses the sleepers by hope, not fear: the day of judgment is at the doors, “the remaining time is short” — so shake off listlessness and press on to the good he has commanded.
Awake from the sleep of sin, of carnal security, of spiritual deadness; the nearer we draw to our journey's end, the quicker and more vigorous our motion should be.
The six vices fall into three pairs — intemperance, uncleanness, contention — shameful even to carnal men; to put on Christ is to be fortified by his Spirit and have God's image renewed in us.
“Put on Christ” says in one word purity, sobriety, peace, and benevolence, and a thousand times more — the whole of our salvation, the most intimate union with him and clothing with all his graces.

external.name: Leon Morris
external.point: The nearness of salvation is not a miscalculated date but the settled conviction that with Christ's death and resurrection the decisive act is past and the consummation is the next thing on God's calendar; the certainty of the dawning day is itself the summons to live now as people of the light.

11.application: Augustine read one sentence of this chapter and rose a changed man. The gospel's ethic is finally not a code to master but a garment to wear: put on Christ each morning as deliberately as you dress, and leave the flesh no quiet corner in which to lay its plans.

_meta.title: Subjection, Love, and the Coming Day
reflection: Paul calls the magistrate “God's servant for your good” (v.4) yet grounds all authority in the God who stands above it (v.1). Where does honouring legitimate authority end and “we must obey God rather than men” (Acts 5:29) begin — and how would you tell the difference?
Love is the one debt never marked paid (v.8). Is there someone toward whom you have quietly decided the account is settled? What would it look like this week to keep the interest paid up?

“Put on the Lord Jesus Christ, and make no provision for the flesh” (v.14) turned Augustine's whole life. Where are you still making provision — leaving a corner stocked for the flesh — and what would it mean to be clothed with Christ there instead?
